headline: Supreme Court upholds NCLT order: Videocon Industries liquidation process to begin
body: The Supreme Court has upheld the National Company Law Tribunal's (NCLT) order to initiate liquidation proceedings against Videocon Industries Limited, marking the end of a prolonged insolvency resolution process. The company, which owes over Rs 64,000 crore to financial creditors including State Bank of India, ICICI Bank, and other lenders, failed to attract any viable resolution plan despite multiple rounds of bidding. The Committee of Creditors (CoC) voted with 87% majority to proceed with liquidation after rejecting the final resolution plan submitted by Twin Star Technologies, which offered only 5% recovery to creditors. The NCLT Mumbai bench appointed a liquidator who will now take control of Videocon's assets, including manufacturing facilities, real estate properties, and brand value. Legal experts estimate the liquidation process could take 2-3 years to complete. Financial creditors are expected to recover only 10-15% of their dues, resulting in significant haircuts. The Videocon case highlights the challenges in resolving large corporate insolvencies where asset values have deteriorated significantly. Founder Venugopal Dhoot and other promoters are being investigated by the Enforcement Directorate for alleged fund diversion and money laundering. The company's employees, numbering around 3,000, face uncertain futures as operations wind down. Industry analysts view this as a cautionary tale about aggressive expansion funded by excessive debt. The liquidation will involve selling assets through e-auctions, with proceeds distributed to creditors according to the priority waterfall defined in the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code. Operational creditors, including small vendors and suppliers owed Rs 2,400 crore, will receive payments only after secured financial creditors are settled. The case has been closely watched as a test of India's insolvency framework, which was introduced in 2016 to expedite resolution of stressed assets. While the IBC has successfully resolved many cases, the Videocon liquidation demonstrates that not all companies can be revived, and liquidation remains the last resort when resolution fails.
